content: Can you help me understand how to implement JWT authentication in a NestJS application? I want to make sure I am doing it the right way and following the best practices.
Sure! I would be happy to help you with implementing JWT authentication in NestJS. This is a very common requirement for modern web applications. Let me walk you through the process step by step.

Great, I appreciate your help. I am currently looking at the NestJS documentation but I am finding it a bit confusing. Could you please give me a clear and concise explanation of the steps I need to take?

Of course! The process of setting up JWT authentication in NestJS involves a few key steps. First, you will need to install the necessary packages. These packages include @nestjs/passport, @nestjs/jwt, passport, and passport-jwt.
I have already installed those packages. What should I do next? I am not sure how to proceed from here.

The next step is to create your AuthModule, AuthService, and JwtStrategy. The AuthModule is responsible for importing all the necessary modules and providers. The AuthService is responsible for the business logic, and the JwtStrategy is responsible for validating the JWT token.
That makes sense. How do I actually create the JwtStrategy? I am not sure what that should look like.

The JwtStrategy extends PassportStrategy and it is where you define how the JWT token should be validated. You inject it with the JWT secret and then implement the validate method which will be called after the token is verified.
I see. And what about protecting routes? How do I make sure that certain routes require authentication?

To protect routes, you use the @UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard) decorator on your controller or individual route methods. The JwtAuthGuard is a guard that uses the JWT strategy to validate the token before allowing access to the route.
Perfect, that is very helpful. Now, one more thing I want to ask about is refresh tokens. Should I implement refresh tokens as well?

Yes, implementing refresh tokens is generally considered a best practice for security. The idea is that your access token has a short expiry time, typically 15 minutes, while your refresh token has a longer expiry time, like 7 days. When the access token expires, the client uses the refresh token to get a new access token.
